question: A Deployment named order-service runs 6 replicas with a livenessProbe on /healthz and a readinessProbe on /readyz. The application takes 90 seconds to initialize — database migration, cache warm-up, connection pool setup. During this window, both /healthz and /readyz return HTTP 503. A rolling update deploys a new image. Walk through the exact startup sequence: which probe terminates the new pod, at what point does the rolling update stall, and why does the startupProbe fix this correctly while increasing initialDelaySeconds on the livenessProbe fails as a long-term solution?
answer: LivenessProbe fires ~3s after pod start; reads HTTP 503, so kubelet restarts the pod in a crash-loop. Pods never become Ready, so maxUnavailable blocks old pod termination and the rollout stalls. Increasing initialDelaySeconds to 120 delays post-crash liveness detection and must be manually retuned per app. startupProbe uses independent failureThreshold to suspend all probes during init, preserving fast runtime detection.
explanation:
## How It Works

The Kubernetes probe system has three distinct health check mechanisms with different purposes. The livenessProbe determines whether the container is running properly and should be restarted if it fails. The readinessProbe determines whether the container should receive traffic through Services. The startupProbe determines whether the container application has finished initializing.

The startupProbe was introduced in Kubernetes 1.16 specifically to solve the slow-init problem. When a startupProbe is defined, the kubelet suspends execution of both the livenessProbe and readinessProbe until the startupProbe succeeds. The startupProbe has its own independent failureThreshold and periodSeconds, allowing it to fail many times without triggering the rapid restart that the livenessProbe would cause.

Once the startupProbe succeeds, the kubelet hands over health checking to the livenessProbe and readinessProbe with their normal, fast failure thresholds. This decouples the startup window from the runtime detection speed — the startupProbe tolerates 300 seconds of initialization (30 failures x 10s) while the livenessProbe still detects failures within 30 seconds during normal operation.

The critical distinction: increasing livenessProbe.initialDelaySeconds to 120 seconds also delays the first check, but it has three flaws. First, initialDelaySeconds applies per pod start — if the pod restarts after running for an hour, the 120-second delay re-applies. Second, it requires manual tuning per application that must be updated when startup time changes. Third, it delays detection of post-restart issues after a crash. The startupProbe solves all three by separating startup tolerance from runtime sensitivity.

## Code Example

```yaml
# Problem: no startupProbe, liveness kills slow-starting pods
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 6
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: order-service:v3
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /readyz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
```

```yaml
# Fixed with startupProbe
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: order-service:v3
    startupProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      failureThreshold: 30
      periodSeconds: 10
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      periodSeconds: 10
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /readyz
        port: 8080
      periodSeconds: 5
```

With the startupProbe:
- The kubelet waits up to 300 seconds for /healthz to return 200
- livenessProbe and readinessProbe are suspended during startup
- No pod restarts occur during initialization
- readinessProbe activates immediately after startupProbe succeeds
- livenessProbe detects runtime failures within 30 seconds

## Key Points

- **startupProbe gates all other probes**: while the startupProbe is still failing, the kubelet skips liveness and readiness checks entirely
- **failureThreshold decoupling**: startupProbe uses a high failureThreshold for minutes-long initialization; livenessProbe uses a low threshold for seconds-fast runtime detection
- **RollingUpdate interaction**: pods stuck in crash-loop-backoff never become Ready, so maxUnavailable prevents old pod termination and the rollout stalls permanently
- **initialDelaySeconds is per-instance**: it reapplies on every container restart, meaning a pod that crashes after hours of runtime faces the full delay again before liveness detection resumes

## Common Mistake

**Setting livenessProbe.initialDelaySeconds to a large value instead of using startupProbe.** This seems to work during the initial rollout but breaks down in three specific scenarios:

1. **Post-restart vulnerability**: If a pod restarts after hours of healthy runtime (e.g., OOM), the initialDelaySeconds timer restarts from zero. During those 120 seconds, a genuinely broken process that starts quickly would not be detected and restarted, wasting resources and potentially serving corrupted data.

2. **Inconsistent startup timing**: If the application startup time varies due to database contention at peak hours, a fixed initialDelaySeconds that was fine at deploy time may be too short under load, triggering liveness failures and cascading restarts that compound the problem.

3. **Configuration drift**: As applications grow and startup time increases, initialDelaySeconds must be manually updated. With startupProbe, operators adjust a single parameter without changing runtime probe sensitivity.

**Why it persists**: The intuitive assumption is that initialDelaySeconds "defers" the liveness check once. In reality, it deferes the check per container start, not per Deployment update. Developers discover this after a crash-loop caused by post-OOM liveness failures that they had avoided during the initial rollout.

## Interview Tip

The interviewer is specifically listening for the distinction between delaying the first check (initialDelaySeconds) and separating the startup window from runtime detection (startupProbe). A good answer mentions that startupProbe was added in Kubernetes 1.16 specifically for this use case. A great answer explains the three failure modes of initialDelaySeconds scaling and connects them to production incidents: deployment stall during rolling update, post-crash restart vulnerability, and load-amplified liveness failures during traffic spikes.

The interviewer will follow up with: "What if the app crashes after initialization completes — does startupProbe help with detection speed?" The correct answer is yes, because after startupProbe succeeds, livenessProbe detects failures at its own fast periodSeconds, while startupProbe is effectively retired until the next container start.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  INIT[Startup State
app starting...
90s init needed] --> NO_SP[No startupProbe]
  INIT --> WITH_SP[With startupProbe]

  NO_SP --> LIVENESS["livenessProbe fires every 10s
(initialDelaySeconds: 3)"]
  LIVENESS --> CHECK503{/healthz returns 503?}
  CHECK503 -->|yes, before 90s done| RESTART["kubelet restarts pod
crash-loop-backoff"]
  RESTART --> INIT

  CHECK503 -->|no, after 90s| READY[Pod becomes Ready
Rolling update continues]

  WITH_SP --> STARTUP["startupProbe fires every 10s
failureThreshold: 30
liveness + readiness suspended"]
  STARTUP --> RETRY{failureCount > 30?}
  RETRY -->|no, still 503| STARTUP
  RETRY -->|no, eventually 200| PASS[startupProbe succeeds]
  PASS --> LIVENESS_2["livenessProbe activates
periodSeconds: 10
fast detection now"]
  PASS --> READINESS_2["readinessProbe activates
/readyz returns 200"]
  READINESS_2 --> TRAFFIC[Pod added to Service
Rolling update progresses]

  style RESTART fill:#f99,stroke:#c00
  style PASS fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
  style NO_SP fill:#ff9,stroke:#c90
  style WITH_SP fill:#9cf,stroke:#06c
